← Aren Vista Math-o-nomicon

Numerical Linear Algebra

  • L01 Vectors, Vector Operations, and the Column Space of a Matrix
  • L02 Matrix Multiplication, Factorization, and Solving Linear Systems
  • L03 LU Factorization, Permutation Matrices, and Partial Pivoting
  • L04 Cholesky Factorization, Block Matrices, and Fundamental Subspaces
  • L05 Parametric Solutions and the Four Fundamental Subspaces
  • L06 The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra, Orthonormal Bases, and Orthogonal Projectors
  • L07 The Gram–Schmidt Algorithm and QR Factorization
  • L08 Orthogonal Matrices: Projectors, Reflectors, and Rotators
  • L09 Least Squares Approximation
  • L10 Least Squares via QR Factorization (Wrap-up)
  • L11 From QR to Eigenvalues: Full QR, Diagonalization, and the Road to SVD
  • L12 The Singular Value Decomposition
  • L13 Working with the SVD: Low-Rank Approximation, Least Squares, and the Pseudoinverse
  • L14 Eigenvalues, Eigenvectors, and the Spectral Theorem
  • L15 Structured Decompositions: Diagonalization, Schur Factorization, and the SVD Revisited
  • L16 Computing Eigenvalues: Iterative Methods and the QR Algorithm
  • L18 Solving Nonlinear Equations: Newton's Method in One and Many Dimensions
  • L19u Newton's Method for Systems, Vector Calculus, and Gauss–Newton

Real Analysis

  • L01 Finite and Infinite Sets
  • L02 Countability, the Real Numbers, and Order
  • Order, Absolute Value, and Completeness of ℝ
  • L04 Completeness, Suprema of Transformed Sets, and the Archimedean Property
  • L05 Existence of √2, Density, and Intervals
  • L06 Nested Intervals and the Foundations of Sequences
  • L07 Boundedness and the Algebra of Limits
  • L08 Algebraic Facts, Limit Laws in Practice, and Monotone Convergence
  • L09 Subsequences and Their Role in Convergence and Divergence
  • L10 The Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem and Limit Superior/Inferior
  • L11 Properties of the Limit Superior
  • L12 The Cauchy Criterion
  • L013 p-Series, Proper Divergence, and Limits of Functions
  • L014 Review: Limsup/Liminf, p-Series, Iterative Sequences, and Subsequences
  • L015 Continuous Functions on Intervals
  • Finding $\delta$ for $\sqrt{x}$, and a Geometrically-Bounded Cauchy Criterion
  • L017 Intermediate Value Theorem
  • Uniform Continuity
  • Quiz Problems and Combination Theorems for Uniform Continuity

to the Math-o-nomicon

Notes taken during a lecture are optimised for keeping up, not for being read later. They fragment across courses, lose their cross-references, and go stale the moment the semester ends — which is exactly when you start needing them, whether that’s for a qualifying exam or for explaining a proof to someone at a tutoring desk.

The Math-o-nomicon rewrites them as one document with a single voice, a single set of conventions, and links that hold across chapters.

Math-o-nomicon

n. What lecture notes become when they are made to answer to a reader instead of a clock.

what’s in it

The spine runs from numerical linear algebra through analysis, with the numerical material carrying most of the weight so far:

  • Orthogonal matrices, QR factorization, and least squares.
  • Conditioning and numerical stability — where the error actually comes from.
  • The SVD, Eckart–Young, and the pseudoinverse.
  • The spectral theorem, Schur factorization, and iterative eigenvalue methods.
  • Newton’s method and Gauss–Newton, tied back to the linear machinery underneath.
  • Real analysis, as the foundation the numerical chapters keep quietly borrowing from.

the conventions

Consistency is the whole feature. Definitions, theorems, proofs, and asides each get a fixed treatment, so a reader learns the visual grammar once and never has to decode it again — you’ve already met one, in fact: the definition block above renders exactly the way every definition in the book does. Diagrams are drawn in TikZ rather than pasted as images, which keeps them editable and keeps the type matching the body text.

Every chapter closes by pointing forward: the section on QR says what it’s setting up for least squares, and the section on Gauss–Newton says which earlier chapter it’s standing on. A theorem in isolation is trivia — the throughline is what makes it a book.

start exploring

The natural place to begin is the first chapter — everything downstream leans on it.

The full chapter list lives in the explorer on the left (or behind the menu, if you’re on mobile), arranged in reading order. If you’d rather see the structure than the sequence, the graph view shows the cross-references directly: the throughline, drawn as an actual line.

what I took away

Writing something down for a stranger is a different operation from writing it down for yourself, and it surfaces every place you were relying on a gap you’d stopped noticing. More than one chapter got rewritten because typesetting the proof made it obvious I couldn’t actually justify a step I’d been waving through for two years.
